description: Default Composer/PHP supply-chain security detection rules. Targets the Laravel-Lang class of attack: autoload.files injection, mutable-tag lock files, and PHP dropper payloads.

rules.title: autoload.files entry detected
rules.description: The composer.json declares files under autoload.files. PHP executes every listed file on every application boot via the Composer autoloader — this was the exact injection vector used in the May 2026 Laravel-Lang supply chain attack. Review each entry carefully.

rules.title: autoload-dev.files entry detected
rules.description: The composer.json declares files under autoload-dev.files. While scoped to development, these files are auto-executed during dev/CI installs and can serve as dropper vectors.

rules.title: composer.lock absent
rules.description: A composer.json exists but no composer.lock was found. Without a lock file, every install resolves dependencies fresh from the registry — a compromised or mutable package tag will silently pull malicious code. The Laravel-Lang attack was undetectable in projects without a committed lock file.

rules.title: Package pinned by tag, not commit SHA
rules.description: One or more packages in composer.lock use a version tag as the sole pin (dist.reference is a version string, not a 40-character commit SHA). Git tags are mutable — the Laravel-Lang attacker force-pushed new malicious commits behind existing tags, bypassing version-pinned installs. Pin to a commit SHA for immutable resolution.

rules.title: Composer script pipes remote content to interpreter
rules.description: A Composer lifecycle script streams remote content directly into php, bash, or another interpreter. This is a critical code-execution risk in CI/CD pipelines.

rules.title: Network download in Composer lifecycle script
rules.description: A Composer lifecycle script invokes a network download utility (curl/wget/Invoke-WebRequest). Downloads during install hooks are a common supply-chain stager technique.

rules.title: Inline PHP execution in Composer script
rules.description: A Composer lifecycle script uses php -r to execute inline PHP code, which can be a vector for obfuscated or encoded payloads.

rules.title: PHP system command execution in autoloaded file
rules.description: An autoloaded PHP file calls a system execution function (system, exec, shell_exec, passthru, proc_open, or backtick operator). Auto-execution on boot combined with shell access is a critical code-execution risk.

rules.title: Base64-encoded eval in autoloaded PHP file
rules.description: An autoloaded PHP file contains eval(base64_decode(...)) or a similar obfuscation chain. This is the canonical PHP dropper signature and matches exactly the payload style used in the Laravel-Lang attack.

rules.title: Remote fetch in autoloaded PHP file
rules.description: An autoloaded PHP file fetches content from a remote URL using file_get_contents, curl, or fsockopen. Combined with auto-execution via autoload.files, this is the exact dropper→stager pattern used in the Laravel-Lang attack.

rules.title: Credential exfiltration patterns in autoloaded PHP file
rules.description: An autoloaded PHP file references common credential sources: environment variables, AWS/GCP/Azure credential paths, SSH key paths, or .env file access. These were all targeted in the Laravel-Lang credential stealer payload.

rules.title: Suspicious C2/exfil endpoint in autoloaded PHP file
rules.description: An autoloaded PHP file references known exfiltration endpoint patterns or suspicious webhook/C2 infrastructure.
